security_audit.summary: All static findings are false positives. The hardcoded URLs at lines 4 and 22 are legitimate GitHub repository links in documentation. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags at lines 3 and 20 incorrectly identify 'ffuf' (Fast Web Fuzzer) as a cryptographic algorithm - it is actually a well-known web fuzzing security tool. No actual security risks detected.

risk_factor_evidence.confidence_reasoning: URLs are GitHub links to source repository, standard documentation practice with no security risk

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (FALSE POSITIVE)
high_findings.description: Scanner incorrectly flagged 'ffuf' as weak cryptographic algorithm. 'ffuf' (Fast Web Fuzzer) is a legitimate open-source web fuzzing tool, not a cryptographic algorithm.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear misinterpretation - ffuf is a well-documented security tool, not a crypto algorithm

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (FALSE POSITIVE)
low_findings.description: Hardcoded GitHub URLs found in documentation are legitimate source references
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard documentation URLs pointing to the project's own GitHub repository

content.value_statement: Ffuf (Fast Web Fuzzer) helps security professionals discover hidden web resources. This skill provides patterns and guidance for integrating ffuf into Claude Code workflows for efficient web reconnaissance.
actual_capabilities: Execute ffuf web fuzzing scans for directory and file discovery
Build optimized ffuf command arguments for various recon scenarios
Parse and analyze ffuf scan results for actionable findings
Integrate ffuf with other reconnaissance tools in the workflow
Configure wordlists and filter options for efficient scanning
limitations: Does not include ffuf binary - must be installed separately
No native output parsing - results require manual analysis

use_cases.title: Directory Discovery Scan
use_cases.description: Scan target web servers for hidden directories and files using common wordlists
use_cases.target_user: Security professionals conducting web application assessments

use_cases.title: Parameter Fuzzing
use_cases.description: Discover hidden parameters in web applications that may reveal functionality or vulnerabilities
use_cases.target_user: Bug bounty hunters and penetration testers

use_cases.title: Subdomain Enumeration
use_cases.description: Identify valid subdomains during the reconnaissance phase of security assessments
use_cases.target_user: Red team members and security auditors

prompt_templates.title: Basic Directory Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Run an ffuf scan to discover directories on [TARGET_URL] using the common wordlist. Show me the results.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick directory enumeration on a target

prompt_templates.title: Filtered Results Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform an ffuf scan on [TARGET_URL] but filter out 403 and 404 status codes. Use a medium-sized wordlist.

prompt_templates.title: Extension Fuzzing
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan [TARGET_URL] for common file extensions like .php, .asp, .txt using ffuf. Exclude 404 responses.

prompt_templates.title: Recursive Directory Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a recursive ffuf scan on [TARGET_URL] starting with the /api/ path. Use the small wordlist and match responses with 200 status.

output_examples.input: Run ffuf directory scan on http://example.com
output_examples.output:
Executing ffuf scan with common wordlist...

Results Summary:
- /admin (Status: 200) - 2.3KB
- /api (Status: 301) - Redirect
- /backup (Status: 403) - Forbidden
- /login (Status: 200) - 5.1KB

Found 4 interesting directories. Recommend checking /admin and /login for further testing.

output_examples.input: Scan for PHP files on example.com
output_examples.output:
Running extension fuzzing with .php extensions...

Discovered endpoints:
- /index.php (200)
- /admin.php (200)
- /api/users.php (200)
- /config.php (403)

4 PHP files found. /config.php returned 403 - may contain sensitive configuration.

best_practices: Start with small wordlists and progressively increase size to avoid overwhelming the target
Use appropriate filters to exclude noisy responses like 404 and 403 status codes
Always obtain proper authorization before scanning any target that you do not own
anti_patterns: Running ffuf without rate limiting which may trigger WAF blocks or DOS protection
Using default wordlists without customization for the specific target technology
Ignoring redirect chains - ffuf follows redirects by default which may miss discovery

faq.question: Do I need to install ffuf separately?
faq.answer: Yes, ffuf must be installed on your system before using this skill. Download it from github.com/ffuf/ffuf.

faq.question: Can this skill scan multiple targets simultaneously?
faq.answer: No, ffuf scans one target at a time. You can run multiple scans sequentially in your workflow.

faq.question: What wordlists are available with ffuf?
faq.answer: Ffuf supports any wordlist file. Common sources include SecLists, Dirb, and custom wordlists you create.

faq.question: Is ffuf safe to use on production systems?
faq.answer: Ffuf sends many requests which may impact server performance. Always get authorization before scanning.

faq.question: Can ffuf discover subdomains?
faq.answer: Yes, ffuf can enumerate subdomains when used with a subdomain wordlist and appropriate DNS settings.

faq.question: Does this skill parse JSON output from ffuf?
faq.answer: This skill provides guidance on parsing results. You may need to use jq or other tools for complex parsing.
